interpretation: Setup only; ECHO must use the same G for fair comparison.
ECHO reward includes additional risk/drop terms; use the sweep to tune ECHO, then compare both methods using external drop ratio and delay.
Same reward-scale issue. ECHO may converge more slowly because of the larger state and semi-Markov transitions.
Raw reward cannot establish superiority across different reward definitions. On a common metric, ECHO is most likely to improve task drop ratio at P=0.7-0.9.
Expected ECHO signature: fewer deadline-infeasible actions, lower fallback/miss rate, and route shifts as deadlines tighten.
ECHO is most likely to improve drop ratio at 4-5 GHz; at 8-9 GHz the methods may converge.

metric: Average reward vs number of agents / traffic
interpretation: Action masking may help under heavy/extreme traffic, but ERT computation and larger state may reduce convergence speed. Compare drop ratio, not raw reward.

metric: Average reward vs number of agents / data rates
interpretation: ECHO should help when route feasibility differs sharply, especially vertical-centric or congested cases, but not necessarily on raw reward.
Likely benefit at P=0.7-0.9; under a 10-s timeout ERT has weak urgency pressure, so gains may be small.
Likely at 3-5 GHz; at 6-7 GHz little separation is expected.
ECHO prioritizes deadline survival, which can trade average delay among successful tasks. It should not be claimed as guaranteed.
Strongest expected advantage at P=0.7 and 0.9 due to ERT queue ordering and deadline-feasible masking.
Strongest expected advantage at 3-5 GHz; the gap should shrink as capacity becomes abundant.
Strongest expected advantage at tight deadlines 1.6-2.0 s; this directly tests ECHO novelty.
ECHO with LSTM should outperform ECHO-NoLSTM under delayed/stale load data. Against HOODIE-LSTM, final delay is plausible but not guaranteed; drop ratio is the stronger target.
